fact: July 16 is exactly 197 days into the year, which means there are only 168 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this is prime time for fireflies to put on their most dazzling light shows - they blink fastest when it's around 80°F!
The sun rises super early around July 16 in places like Alaska - we're talking 4:30 AM early, giving kids almost 19 hours of daylight to play outside!
Cicadas are screaming their loudest around this date because the heat makes their wing muscles work overtime - they can buzz at 120 decibels!
In Australia and other Southern Hemisphere countries, July 16 falls smack in the middle of winter, so penguins are huddling together for warmth while we're sweating!
This date falls right in Cancer season, making it one of the most 'crabby' days of the year according to the zodiac!
Sunflowers in the Northern Hemisphere are typically at their tallest around July 16 - some tower over 12 feet high, which is taller than a basketball hoop!
The ocean water temperature peaks around this time in many coastal areas, making it perfect for jellyfish blooms that can stretch for miles!
July 16 is roughly 25 days after the summer solstice, which means each day is getting about 2 minutes shorter in the Northern Hemisphere - summer is already sneaking away!
Mosquitoes are at their most annoying around this date because the hot, humid weather makes them breed like crazy - a single female can lay 300 eggs at once!
In many places, July 16 marks the peak of 'dog days of summer' when the air gets so thick and sticky that even dogs don't want to run around!
The full moon closest to July 16 is called the Buck Moon because male deer are growing their biggest, most impressive antlers right now!
